skill.name: ru
skill.description: RU documents a Bash CLI for synchronizing many Git repositories, reviewing issues and PRs, and orchestrating AI-assisted repository cleanup.
skill.summary: Synchronize and review many Git repositories with AI-assisted automation.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and environment-token patterns. Most command hits are Markdown examples and intended repo-management behavior, but the skill also documents remote pipe-to-shell installation and AI workflows that can commit and push changes. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly to Shell
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The installation example pipes a remote script from GitHub directly into bash. This creates supply-chain risk because the fetched script executes before the user can inspect or pin it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit on line 481. It is documented as installation guidance, so the risk is confirmed even though malicious intent is not proven.

high_findings.title: AI Agents Can Commit and Push Repository Changes
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The documented workflows spawn Claude Code sessions, stage files, create commits, and optionally push changes or create releases. This is powerful automation that can alter many repositories if misconfigured or misused.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill text directly describes spawning AI sessions and applying changes. It also documents optional push and release behavior, which confirms broad write impact.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Execution Is Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: RU is designed to run git, gh, curl, tmux or ntm, test, and lint commands across many repositories. This is legitimate for a repo updater, but it requires trusted configuration and user oversight.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and integration table are explicit. The context is operational documentation, so this is elevated capability risk rather than hidden execution.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Access Includes Repo State and Logs
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents configuration under hidden user directories, per-repo configuration files, logs, and state files. This is expected for the tool, but it can expose private repository paths and operational history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and log commands are documented directly. No credential theft is shown, but the filesystem surface is broad enough to warrant a medium concern.

low_findings.title: Secret File Patterns Are Denylist Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged secret filenames, but the line lists files that should never be committed. The context is a safety guardrail rather than credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The table heading says the files are denied from commits, and the following section documents secret scanning. This strongly indicates a protective use.

low_findings.title: GitHub Token Mention Is Authentication Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The GH_TOKEN references document authentication setup and troubleshooting. I found no evidence that the token is read and sent to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references appear in environment variable and troubleshooting tables. There is no accompanying exfiltration or obfuscation pattern in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Finding Is Documentation Placeholder
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The scanner flagged a shortened path example, but it appears in a per-repository configuration filename. The same section describes path security validation elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged path is inside a YAML example and not executable code. The context is configuration documentation, so exploitability is low.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends fetching an installer with curl and piping it to bash. Users should inspect or pin installer contents before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pattern is present in the installation block. This is a known dangerous pattern even when used by legitimate tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Multi-Repository Write Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes automated staging, commits, pushes, tags, and releases across repositories. This can create large-scale unintended changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow text explicitly names commit, push, tagging, and release operations. The risk depends on user configuration but is clearly present.

content.value_statement: Managing many Git repositories creates missed updates, conflicts, and unfinished local changes. RU provides a structured workflow for sync, review planning, and AI-assisted cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Explains RU commands for syncing configured Git repositories.
Documents dry-run, resume, JSON, and parallel sync options.
Describes review planning for GitHub issues and pull requests.
Outlines agent-sweep workflows for dirty repositories.
Lists configuration paths, environment variables, and troubleshooting steps.
Covers quality gates, secret scanning modes, and denylist behavior.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not include the RU executable.
Automated commits and pushes require careful human review.
GitHub operations depend on gh authentication and repository permissions.
The install example uses a remote shell script, which needs independent verification.

use_cases.title: Keep Personal Repositories Current
use_cases.description: Use RU guidance to check status, sync, and resume updates across many local clones.

use_cases.title: Plan AI-Assisted Review Work
use_cases.description: Use the review workflow to prioritize issues and pull requests before applying changes.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Dirty Worktrees
use_cases.description: Use agent-sweep concepts to inspect uncommitted changes, create plans, and commit approved files.

prompt_templates.title: Check Repository Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the RU skill to explain how I can check the status of all configured repositories without modifying files.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Sync
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the RU skill to create a safe sync plan with dry-run, parallelism, and resume behavior for my repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Review Dirty Repositories
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the RU skill to outline an agent-sweep workflow that reviews dirty repositories without pushing changes automatically.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Maintainer Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the RU skill to design a maintainer workflow for issue review, quality gates, secret scanning, and manual approval before push.

output_examples.input: I need to sync 40 repositories safely.
output_examples.output: Start with a dry run, inspect skipped and diverged repositories, then run a limited parallel sync. Keep force update disabled unless each repository is reviewed.

output_examples.input: I have many dirty repositories and want AI help.
output_examples.output: Run a preview first, filter to a small repository set, review the generated plan, and keep push or release steps disabled until changes pass tests.

output_examples.input: I want to troubleshoot skipped repositories.
output_examples.output: Check the JSON status output and latest logs. Look for missing upstream branches, detached heads, shallow clones, or in-progress merge and rebase states.

best_practices: Use dry-run and JSON status before any write operation.
Keep secret scanning enabled and review denylist behavior before committing.
Require manual approval before push, tag, or release steps.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell without inspection.
Do not run agent-sweep across all private repositories on the first attempt.
Do not use force update or automatic push for repositories with unreviewed changes.

faq.question: Does this skill include the RU program?
faq.answer: No. It documents how to use RU and its workflows, but the reviewed skill contains only SKILL.md.

faq.question: Can RU modify repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows can stage, commit, push, tag, and create releases when enabled.

faq.question: Is the skill safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. It references private repo lists, logs, GitHub authentication, and AI-assisted write workflows.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill documents remote shell installation and broad automation that can change many repositories.

faq.question: Were prompt injection attempts found?
faq.answer: No evidence found in SKILL.md for prompt injection phrases or attempts to override review instructions.

faq.question: How should users start safely?
faq.answer: Begin with status checks and dry runs, then test one or two low-risk repositories before enabling writes.
